dependencies.description: Requires IP reachability between BFD peers
dependencies.name: IP connectivity

dependencies.description: Both sides must have BFD configured with compatible parameters
dependencies.name: BFD enabled on both endpoints

dependencies.description: Routing protocol must be configured to use BFD for failure detection
dependencies.name: Client protocol integration

dependencies.description: Sub-100ms timers typically require ASIC/hardware offload for reliable operation
dependencies.name: Hardware offload for aggressive timers

dependencies.description: BFD in SONiC runs as the bfdd process inside the 'bgp' Docker container via FRR. bfdd manages the BFD FSM, sends and receives Control packets, and notifies registered clients (bgpd, ospfd, zebra) on session state changes via the FRR inter-process communication (Zserv/ZAPI).
dependencies.name: FRR bfdd

dependencies.description: FRR zebra acts as the central routing daemon and receives BFD session notifications from bfdd via ZAPI. When bfdd declares a session Down, zebra triggers next-hop tracking updates and notifies bgpd and ospfd. Zebra must be running for BFD notifications to propagate to routing protocols.
dependencies.name: zebra

dependencies.description: BGP uses BFD for sub-second peer failure detection. When 'neighbor <ip> bfd' is configured in FRR, bgpd registers the peer with bfdd. On BFD session Down, bgpd immediately tears down the BGP session without waiting for the Hold Timer to expire.
dependencies.name: bgpd (optional client)

dependencies.description: OSPF uses BFD for sub-second neighbor failure detection. When BFD is enabled on an OSPF interface, ospfd registers the neighbor with bfdd. On BFD Down, ospfd immediately triggers SPF recalculation and route updates.
dependencies.name: ospfd (optional client)

dependencies.description: On platforms supporting hardware-offloaded BFD, orchagent programs BFD session objects into ASIC_DB via SAI. The bfdorch module inside orchagent handles BFD SAI object lifecycle. Hardware offload is optional -- software BFD in bfdd operates independently of orchagent.
dependencies.name: orchagent (swss container)

dependencies.description: BFD Control packets are delivered to bfdd via the CPU path and are subject to CoPP rate limiting. The default BFD CoPP class rate must be high enough to sustain the configured BFD packet rate across all sessions. CoPP drops are the most common cause of BFD false positives in SONiC.
dependencies.name: CoPP (Control Plane Policing)

dependencies.description: Source of truth for BFD configuration: BFD_PEER_SINGLE_HOP and BFD_PEER_MULTI_HOP tables. frrcfgd or bgpcfgd reads these to configure bfdd via vtysh.
dependencies.name: CONFIG_DB (Redis DB 4)

dependencies.description: BFD session state is published here in BFD_SESSION_TABLE. Other SONiC components and monitoring tools read this table to determine current BFD session health. Updated by bfdd via the FRR BFD state change notification path.
dependencies.name: STATE_DB (Redis DB 6)

failure_modes.description: BFD session fails to reach UP state, remaining stuck in DOWN or INIT

resolutions.action: Verify BFD enabled on both ends: 'show bfd neighbors' on both sides
Verify BFD enabled on both ends: 'show bfd neighbors' on both sides
Verify BFD enabled on both ends: 'show bfd neighbors' on both sides
Check for ACL blocking UDP 3784: 'show ip access-lists'
Check for ACL blocking UDP 3784: 'show ip access-lists'
Check for ACL blocking UDP 3784: 'show ip access-lists'
Verify client protocol has BFD enabled: e.g., 'ip ospf bfd', 'neighbor <ip> fall-over bfd'
Verify client protocol has BFD enabled: e.g., 'ip ospf bfd', 'neighbor <ip> fall-over bfd'
Verify client protocol has BFD enabled: e.g., 'ip ospf bfd', 'neighbor <ip> fall-over bfd'
Check BFD mode matches: both single-hop or both multi-hop
Check BFD mode matches: both single-hop or both multi-hop
Check BFD mode matches: both single-hop or both multi-hop
On port-channels: verify per-link BFD or micro-BFD is configured correctly
On port-channels: verify per-link BFD or micro-BFD is configured correctly
On port-channels: verify per-link BFD or micro-BFD is configured correctly

root_causes.cause: BFD not enabled on both ends of the link
ACL blocking UDP 3784/4784
BFD timer mismatch preventing negotiation (rare — BFD negotiates)
Asymmetric BFD support (one side hardware, other software with incompatible minimum timers)
Client protocol not configured to use BFD
ECMP/LAG causing BFD packets to take different path than data traffic

symptoms: show bfd neighbors shows DOWN or INIT state
Associated routing protocol not converging
Client protocol (OSPF/BGP) not registering BFD session

failure_modes.description: BFD session repeatedly transitions between UP and DOWN states

resolutions.action: Move to hardware-offloaded BFD if using software mode
Move to hardware-offloaded BFD if using software mode
Move to hardware-offloaded BFD if using software mode
Increase detect multiplier (e.g., 3→5) to tolerate transient loss
Increase detect multiplier (e.g., 3→5) to tolerate transient loss

root_causes.cause: Aggressive timer settings on congested control-plane
Software-based BFD hitting CPU limits under load
Packet loss on link exceeding detect multiplier tolerance
Interface micro-outages (e.g., optics degradation)
Asymmetric forwarding — BFD and data on different paths
QoS not prioritizing BFD packets

Associated routing protocol flapping with BFD
Traffic disruption during BFD flaps

Verify BFD client registration: 'show bfd neighbors detail' — check registered protocols
Verify BFD client registration: 'show bfd neighbors detail' — check registered protocols
Reconfigure BFD on the routing protocol
Reconfigure BFD on the routing protocol

Ensure BFD source/destination matches routing protocol peering addresses
Ensure BFD source/destination matches routing protocol peering addresses

root_causes.cause: BFD not registered as client by the routing protocol
BFD session exists but routing protocol not subscribed to it
Different BFD session (wrong source/dest) than routing protocol expects
BFD dampening delaying notification to clients

failure_modes.scenario: BFD not triggering routing protocol failover
symptoms: BFD goes DOWN but routing protocol adjacency stays up
Failover takes dead-timer time instead of BFD detect time
show bfd neighbors shows DOWN but OSPF/BGP neighbor shows FULL/Established

failure_modes.description: The BFD session is configured on both ends but never leaves the Down state. bfdd is sending Control packets but not receiving any from the peer, or the received packets are being dropped before reaching bfdd. This is the most common BFD failure in SONiC deployments and is almost always caused by IP reachability problems, CoPP rate limiting, or misconfiguration of the peer address.

resolutions.action: Verify IP reachability: 'ping <peer-ip> -I <src-interface> -c 5'
Capture BFD packets: 'sudo tcpdump -i <interface> udp port 3784 -c 20' -- check for inbound packets
Check CoPP: 'show copp config' and 'show copp statistics' -- look for BFD class drop counters
Verify bfdd on peer: confirm FRR bfdd process is running on the remote device
Check source IP: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c show bfd peers' shows Local address being used
Verify VRF: ensure BFD session is configured in the same VRF on both ends
Check CONFIG_DB: 'redis-cli -n 4 KEYS BFD*' to confirm BFD configuration is present

root_causes.cause: Peer IP address unreachable -- no route to peer, interface down, or ARP/ND not resolved
CoPP (Control Plane Policing) dropping BFD UDP packets before they reach bfdd -- default CoPP rate may be too low
Firewall or ACL on the peer device blocking UDP port 3784
bfdd not running on the peer side -- peer does not have BFD configured or its bfdd process crashed
Wrong source IP -- bfdd is sourcing packets from an IP the peer does not recognize and is dropping
VRF mismatch -- BFD session configured in wrong VRF on one end
TTL issue for single-hop BFD -- BFD Control packets must be sent with TTL=255 (RFC 5881); intermediate routing would decrement TTL and peer drops packets with TTL != 255

failure_modes.scenario: BFD session not establishing -- stuck in Down state
symptoms: 'show bfd peers' shows session state as 'down' with no uptime
'docker exec bgp vtysh -c show bfd peers' shows State: Down for the peer
redis-cli -n 6 HGETALL shows state=down in BFD_SESSION_TABLE
BFD packets visible outbound in tcpdump on UDP port 3784 but no inbound packets seen
BGP or OSPF that depends on BFD never comes up or stays in degraded state

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers'
verify_commands.confirms_if: State: Down with no uptime confirms session has never established
verify_commands.look_for: State field -- Down with Diagnostic 'No Diagnostic' means never received a packet; 'Control Detection Time Expired' means was Up before

verify_commands.confirms_if: Outbound but no inbound BFD packets confirms peer is not responding -- unreachable or bfdd not running on peer
verify_commands.look_for: Outbound BFD packets from local IP to peer IP; absence of inbound packets from peer confirms one-way or no path

verify_commands.confirms_if: Non-zero BFD drop counter in CoPP statistics confirms control plane policing is discarding BFD packets
verify_commands.look_for: BFD trap class drop counter -- non-zero drops indicate CoPP is rate-limiting BFD packets

verify_commands.confirms_if: 100% packet loss confirms IP connectivity is broken, explaining why BFD cannot establish
verify_commands.look_for: Packet loss or unreachable -- any loss indicates Layer 3 reachability issue

verify_commands.confirms_if: Wrong local_addr or peer_addr in STATE_DB confirms misconfiguration of the BFD session parameters
verify_commands.look_for: state field value -- 'down' confirms current state; check local_addr and peer_addr for correctness

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers counters'
verify_commands.confirms_if: TX incrementing with zero RX confirms local bfdd is sending but receiving nothing from peer
verify_commands.look_for: Control packet TX count increasing but RX count at zero

failure_modes.description: BFD sessions that were stable begin going Down and coming back Up (flapping) during periods of high CPU utilization on the SONiC device. The BFD detection timer expires because bfdd cannot process or send Control packets fast enough when the CPU is saturated. This generates spurious BGP or OSPF session drops that are not caused by actual forwarding failures. This is one of the most operationally damaging BFD failure modes in production.

resolutions.action: Increase BFD timers to reduce sensitivity: change to 300ms tx/rx with multiplier 3 (900ms detection) or 500ms with multiplier 3
Increase detect-multiplier from 3 to 5 before increasing intervals -- less aggressive detection
Check CPU load: 'top' and 'docker stats' during a flap event to identify the competing process
Check bfdd memory and CPU inside bgp container: 'docker exec bgp top -p $(docker exec bgp pgrep bfdd)'
Reduce BFD session count if running many sessions -- consolidate where protocol-level detection is acceptable
Check CoPP BFD class rates: 'show copp config' -- increase BFD CoPP rate if platform supports it
Do NOT set BFD timers below 100ms on most SONiC platforms without validating control plane capacity first

root_causes.cause: bfdd process starved of CPU time due to other processes in the bgp container (bgpd, zebra, fpmsyncd) consuming CPU during route churn
BFD timer intervals set too aggressively (below 100ms) for the platform's control plane capacity
Linux scheduler not prioritizing bfdd -- BFD runs at normal process priority in FRR
CoPP burst absorbing BFD packets in bursts then dropping under sustained load
Large BGP UPDATE storms triggering zebra and fpmsyncd activity that starves bfdd
System memory pressure causing swap usage and increasing timer jitter
Route scale too large -- thousands of BFD sessions consuming disproportionate CPU on timer processing

failure_modes.scenario: BFD false positives from CPU overload -- session flaps under load
symptoms: 'show bfd peers' shows intermittent Down/Up transitions during heavy traffic or route churn
BGP or OSPF sessions drop coincidentally with high CPU periods
BFD session diagnostic code shows 'Control Detection Time Expired' in logs
'docker stats' shows high CPU in bgp or other containers
syslog shows BFD session down/up events correlating with route table updates or other CPU-intensive events
Sessions with aggressive timers (50-100ms) are more affected than those with 300ms+ timers

verify_commands.confirms_if: High CPU in bgp container during BFD flap events confirms resource contention is causing false positives
verify_commands.look_for: CPU % for bgp container -- high CPU (>70%) during flap events correlates with timer starvation

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Detection time expired diagnostic without link-level events confirms timer starvation, not actual path failure
verify_commands.look_for: Diagnostic code field -- 'Control Detection Time Expired' on a session that should have stable forwarding

verify_commands.confirms_if: Flap events correlating with BGP route churn or other CPU-intensive periods confirm false positive hypothesis
verify_commands.look_for: Repeated BFD session down/up events with short durations between them; timestamps correlating with other events

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers counters'
verify_commands.confirms_if: RX count continuing to increment after session Down confirms packets are arriving but detection timer fired before processing
verify_commands.look_for: Control RX count -- check if RX packets are being received but processed late (session goes Down then quickly Up)

verify_commands.confirms_if: BFD CoPP drops during high-load periods confirm control plane policing is contributing to false positives
verify_commands.look_for: BFD trap class drops -- elevated drops confirm control plane is not keeping up with BFD packet rate

failure_modes.description: BFD echo mode is configured locally but the peer's forwarding plane is not looping back echo packets. Echo mode tests the forwarding path end-to-end by sending packets that the peer should loop back without processing at the BFD protocol layer. If the peer does not support echo loopback, or if an intermediate device is consuming the echo packets, the echo detection timer will expire and the session will go Down even though regular Control packet exchange is working fine.

resolutions.action: Disable echo mode: 'no bfd echo-mode' in FRR bfdd config or remove echo-mode from BFD peer config
Verify peer echo support: check peer documentation and BFD Required Min Echo RX Interval in received Control packets (should be non-zero)
Capture echo traffic: 'sudo tcpdump -i any udp port 3785 -c 10' to check if echo packets return
Use standard Control packet mode for detection -- adequate for most deployments when timer intervals are set correctly
If echo is required: ensure both endpoints have echo mode enabled and no ACL blocks UDP 3785

root_causes.cause: Peer device does not support BFD echo loopback -- not all implementations loop back echo packets
Peer firewall or ACL blocking UDP port 3785 (BFD echo port) while allowing 3784
Intermediate device consuming echo packets instead of forwarding them back
Required Min Echo RX Interval set to 0 on peer -- peer has advertised it will not support echo
Echo packets being routed to a different path than the control packets, breaking the loopback
Virtual environments (KVM, Docker, virtualized SONiC) often do not support echo mode -- hypervisor does not loop back packets

failure_modes.scenario: BFD echo mode failures -- echo packets not looped back by peer
symptoms: BFD session goes Down with diagnostic 'Echo Function Failed'
Regular BFD Control packet exchange works ('show bfd peers counters' shows RX incrementing)
Session goes Up briefly then Down again on echo failure
Peer device documentation or configuration does not mention echo mode support
Echo packets visible outbound in tcpdump on UDP port 3785 but no return traffic seen

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Echo Function Failed diagnostic with Control packets still being exchanged confirms echo mode misconfiguration or peer incompatibility
verify_commands.look_for: Diagnostic code 'Echo Function Failed' -- confirms echo mode is the cause, not Control packet loss

verify_commands.confirms_if: No return echo packets confirms peer does not support or is not configured for echo loopback
verify_commands.look_for: Outbound echo packets; absence of return echo packets from peer confirms peer is not looping them back

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers counters'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Echo TX without matching Echo RX confirms echo packets are being sent but not returned by peer
verify_commands.look_for: Echo TX incrementing but Echo RX at zero or not incrementing

verify_commands.confirms_if: Required Min Echo RX Interval = 0 in peer's Control packets confirms peer explicitly disabled echo support
verify_commands.look_for: Required Min Echo RX Interval field in captured BFD Control packets from peer -- value of 0 means peer disables echo

failure_modes.description: Multi-hop BFD (RFC 5883) sessions configured between non-adjacent peers fail to establish or flap intermittently. Unlike single-hop BFD, multi-hop uses UDP port 4784 and does not enforce the TTL=255 rule. Common in SONiC deployments where BGP loopback-based peering uses multi-hop BFD for liveness detection across multiple hops. Failures are often due to routing asymmetry, source address mismatches, or TTL configuration errors.

resolutions.action: Verify multi-hop configuration: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c show bfd peers' -- confirm multihop flag is set
Explicitly set local-address to the loopback IP: 'bfd peer <remote-loopback> local-address <local-loopback> multihop'
Capture on UDP 4784: 'sudo tcpdump -i any udp port 4784 -c 20' to verify packets are reaching bfdd
Check routing for the BFD peer address: 'ip route get <peer-loopback-ip> from <local-loopback-ip>'
Verify symmetric routing -- trace path in both directions to ensure same devices are traversed
For TTL issues: check intermediate device ACLs and TTL decrements along the path

root_causes.cause: Source address mismatch -- bfdd is sourcing multi-hop BFD from an interface IP instead of the loopback IP configured as the peer source
Routing asymmetry -- forward path and return path go through different devices; return path does not reach the correct bfdd listening socket
TTL too low -- multi-hop BFD packets being dropped by intermediate routers with TTL exhausted
Missing multihop keyword in FRR peer config -- bfdd treating the session as single-hop and enforcing wrong port/TTL rules
Firewall blocking UDP port 4784 while allowing 3784
Static route to reach the BFD peer loopback is missing -- BFD session comes up before routing converges
Profile misconfiguration -- local-address not set to the correct loopback interface

failure_modes.scenario: Multi-hop BFD failures -- TTL or source address mismatches
symptoms: Multi-hop BFD session stuck in Down state despite IP reachability between loopbacks
Session goes Down intermittently when routing changes temporarily alter the path between loopbacks
BFD peer shows Down but 'ping <loopback-ip> source <local-loopback>' succeeds
Diagnostic 'Path Down' or 'Concatenated Path Down' in BFD session status
'show bfd peers' shows multi-hop session never reaching Up

verify_commands.confirms_if: Outbound packets but no inbound on port 4784 confirms multi-hop BFD packets are not reaching local bfdd
verify_commands.look_for: BFD multi-hop packets on port 4784 -- source and destination IP addresses; absence of inbound packets

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Wrong local address in session details confirms source address mismatch causing packets to go to wrong destination
verify_commands.look_for: Multi-hop field in session details; Local address field -- must match the intended source loopback

verify_commands.confirms_if: No route or unexpected path confirms routing issue preventing multi-hop BFD packets from reaching peer
verify_commands.look_for: Route exists and shows expected next-hop and outgoing interface

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers counters'
verify_commands.confirms_if: TX without RX confirms packets are being sent but return path is broken
verify_commands.look_for: TX count incrementing but RX count at zero for multi-hop session

verify_commands.confirms_if: Asymmetric paths or dropped hops confirm routing asymmetry or TTL exhaustion is breaking multi-hop BFD
verify_commands.look_for: Path taken and hop count; check for asymmetry compared to reverse traceroute

failure_modes.description: Two BFD peers are configured with significantly different timer intervals. BFD timer negotiation results in using the higher of the two desired-min-tx values and the higher of the two required-min-rx values, but the detection timer is calculated based on the remote peer's detect-multiplier and the negotiated interval. Asymmetric configuration can result in one side detecting failures much faster than the other, or in unexpected detection times that operators do not anticipate.

resolutions.action: Check negotiated timers: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c show bfd peers' -- look for TX/RX interval fields showing negotiated values
Align timer configuration on both ends to the same desired-min-tx and required-min-rx
Calculate actual detection time: detection = remote detect-multiplier * negotiated RX interval
After changing timers, verify both ends have updated: check 'show bfd peers' on both devices
For sub-second detection requirements, set both required-min-rx to the target interval -- this enforces the upper bound regardless of peer config

root_causes.cause: Local desired-min-tx (50ms) lower than remote required-min-rx (300ms) -- negotiation forces TX at 300ms, giving 900ms detection with multiplier 3
Different detection multipliers on each end -- each end independently calculates its own detection timeout using remote detect-mult
Configuration applied to only one end after a timer change -- peer still has old timer values
Platform hardware limitation on one end setting a higher required-min-rx than configured
FRR bfdd overriding configured timers based on platform capability limits

failure_modes.scenario: Asymmetric timer negotiation causing unexpected session behavior
symptoms: BFD session is Up but detection time is much longer or shorter than expected
'show bfd peers' shows negotiated TX interval different from configured desired-min-tx
One end detects the peer as Down much faster than the other end (asymmetric detection)
BFD session degrades after configuration change on one peer without changing the other
Operators configure 50ms intervals expecting 150ms detection but actual detection is 900ms due to peer's 300ms required-min-rx

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Negotiated intervals different from configured values confirms timer negotiation has resolved to a different value due to peer constraints
verify_commands.look_for: Negotiated TX Interval and Negotiated RX Interval fields -- compare against configured desired-min-tx and required-min-rx

verify_commands.confirms_if: Packet interval larger than configured TX interval confirms peer's required-min-rx is constraining the negotiated rate
verify_commands.look_for: Timestamp delta between consecutive BFD packets -- actual TX interval can be measured from packet captures

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers counters'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Packet reception rate lower than expected confirms negotiated interval is longer than locally configured
verify_commands.look_for: Control packet RX rate -- compare expected rate (1/interval) against actual reception rate

verify_commands.confirms_if: Mismatch between local and negotiated intervals in STATE_DB confirms asymmetric negotiation
verify_commands.look_for: negotiated_transmit_interval and negotiated_receive_interval fields vs local_transmit_interval and local_receive_interval

failure_modes.description: Some SONiC-supported ASIC platforms (e.g., certain Broadcom and Mellanox/NVIDIA platforms) support hardware-offloaded BFD, where the ASIC processes BFD Control packets and manages the detection timer in hardware without involving the control plane CPU. When hardware offload is misconfigured, not initialized properly, or encounters a SAI programming error, BFD sessions may not establish, may silently fail to detect forwarding failures, or may generate false positives from the hardware detection path.

resolutions.action: Check orchagent BFD SAI errors: 'docker logs swss 2>&1 | grep -i bfd | tail -50'
Check ASIC_DB for BFD session objects: 'redis-cli -n 1 KEYS "ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_BFD*"'
Verify STATE_DB BFD status: 'redis-cli -n 6 KEYS "BFD_SESSION_TABLE*"'
Check if hardware BFD is supported on this platform: review platform SAI capabilities
If hardware offload failing, disable hardware BFD and fall back to software: consult platform documentation
Count hardware BFD sessions: 'redis-cli -n 1 KEYS "ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_BFD_SESSION*" | wc -l'

root_causes.cause: SAI BFD object creation failure -- orchagent attempted to program hardware BFD session but SAI returned error
Hardware BFD table full -- ASIC supports limited number of hardware BFD sessions (platform-specific)
bfdd not signaling session to orchagent for hardware offload -- FRR BFD hardware offload integration not triggered
Mismatch between software BFD configuration and hardware BFD capabilities (e.g., timer resolution below ASIC minimum)
syncd SAI library version does not support BFD SAI objects for this platform
BFD session in a non-default VRF and hardware VRF-aware BFD not supported on this platform
orchagent BFD orchestrator not initialized or crashed

failure_modes.scenario: Hardware BFD offload failures on platforms that support it
symptoms: BFD sessions configured but ASIC is not generating hardware BFD packets -- no UDP 3784 traffic seen at line rate
Hardware BFD detection events not propagating to bfdd or to BGP/OSPF
BFD sessions show Up in software ('show bfd peers') but hardware forwarding detection is broken
orchagent logs show SAI errors when creating BFD session objects
Platform supports hardware BFD but sessions are falling back to software mode unexpectedly
Very high session counts causing hardware BFD table exhaustion

verify_commands.command: docker logs swss 2>&1 | grep -i bfd | tail -50
verify_commands.confirms_if: SAI errors in orchagent logs confirm hardware BFD programming failed
verify_commands.look_for: SAI errors creating BFD session objects -- SAI_STATUS_FAILURE, SAI_STATUS_TABLE_FULL, or unsupported attribute errors

verify_commands.confirms_if: Empty result when BFD sessions exist in software confirms hardware offload programming did not complete
verify_commands.look_for: BFD session keys present in ASIC_DB -- one per configured BFD session that is hardware-offloaded

verify_commands.confirms_if: Count at or near platform limit confirms hardware BFD table exhaustion
verify_commands.look_for: Count of hardware BFD sessions; compare against platform hardware BFD session limit

verify_commands.confirms_if: bfdorch errors confirm the orchagent BFD orchestrator failed to program hardware sessions
verify_commands.look_for: BFD orchestrator initialization and session creation log messages; errors indicate offload path failures

verify_commands.confirms_if: offload_type showing 'none' or software when hardware is expected confirms hardware offload is not active
verify_commands.look_for: offload_type field if present -- indicates whether hardware or software BFD is active for this session

messages.description: Carries session state and negotiation parameters
key_fields: Flags (Poll, Final, Control Plane Independent, Authentication, Demand, Multipoint)
messages.name: BFD Control Packet

messages.description: The primary BFD protocol message used for session establishment, liveness detection, and parameter negotiation. Sent at the negotiated interval in both directions. Contains FSM state, discriminators, timer values, and flag bits for Poll/Final and Demand mode.
key_fields: Diag (diagnostic code -- reason for last state change: 0=No Diagnostic, 1=Control Detection Time Expired, 2=Echo Function Failed, 3=Neighbor Signaled Session Down, 4=Forwarding Plane Reset, 5=Path Down, 6=Concatenated Path Down, 7=Administratively Down, 8=Reverse Concatenated Path Down)
C (Control Plane Independent -- set if forwarding continues independently of control plane)
Length (total packet length, typically 24 bytes without auth)
My Discriminator (locally assigned unique session ID)
Your Discriminator (remote discriminator learned from peer; 0 before session established)
Required Min Echo RX Interval (microseconds; 0 to disable echo mode)
messages.name: BFD Control
messages.when_sent: Periodically at the negotiated TX interval when session is Down, Init, or Up; immediately on state change

messages.description: Optional echo mode packets sent by the local system and expected to be looped back by the peer's forwarding plane (not processed by the peer's BFD stack). Tests the forwarding path independently of the control plane. Sent on UDP port 3785. Not universally supported -- some peers or platforms do not loop back echo packets.
key_fields: Local Discriminator (used to match returned echo to the correct session)
Payload (arbitrary -- looped back unchanged by peer forwarding plane)
messages.name: BFD Echo
messages.when_sent: When echo mode is enabled and the session is Up; sent at the echo TX interval

purpose: Bidirectional Forwarding Detection — provides sub-second failure detection for any transport protocol

states.description: Session is down or just initialized
states.name: DOWN

states.description: Local side is up, waiting for peer to confirm
states.name: INIT

states.description: Session fully operational, forwarding path verified
states.name: UP

timers.description: Minimum interval between transmitted BFD control packets
timers.name: DesiredMinTxInterval

os_defaults.config_path: CONFIG_DB BFD_PEER_SINGLE_HOP|<vrf>|<peer-ip> or FRR: 'bfd peer <ip>: transmit-interval <ms>'

timers.tuning_advice: Lower for faster detection; must consider CPU/ASIC capability. Hardware-offloaded BFD can go to 50ms, software BFD typically 50-100ms minimum.

timers.description: Minimum receive interval this system can handle
timers.name: RequiredMinRxInterval

os_defaults.config_path: CONFIG_DB BFD_PEER_SINGLE_HOP|<vrf>|<peer-ip> or FRR: 'bfd peer <ip>: receive-interval <ms>'

timers.tuning_advice: Set to match or slightly higher than tx interval

timers.description: Number of missed packets before declaring session down
timers.name: DetectMultiplier
timers.on_expiry: Session declared DOWN after (DetectMultiplier × negotiated interval) with no packet
timers.tuning_advice: Higher values tolerate more packet loss but slow detection. Typical: 3 (e.g., 3 × 50ms = 150ms detect time)

timers.description: Number of consecutive missed Control packets before the session is declared Down. The detection timer = detection-multiplier * negotiated-rx-interval. A multiplier of 3 with 300ms intervals gives a 900ms detection time. Sent in every Control packet so the peer knows how many packets it can miss before being declared down.
timers.name: Detection Multiplier
timers.on_expiry: Detection timer fires: session declared Down; registered protocols (BGP, OSPF) notified immediately

os_defaults.config_path: CONFIG_DB BFD_PEER_SINGLE_HOP|<vrf>|<peer-ip> or FRR: 'bfd peer <ip>: detect-multiplier <n>'

timers.tuning_advice: Use multiplier 3 as the default. Multiplier 1 is aggressive and causes false positives on any single dropped packet (common under CPU load). For noisy or high-latency paths, increase multiplier rather than increasing interval.

timers.default_rfc: Implicit: uses current TX interval for retransmission
timers.description: When BFD parameters change (e.g., interval renegotiation), bfdd initiates a Poll sequence by setting the Poll (P) bit in Control packets. The peer responds with a Final (F) bit. The new parameters take effect after the Final is received. A timeout during Poll causes the session to reset.
timers.name: Poll Sequence Timer
timers.on_expiry: Poll sequence retransmit or session Down if no Final received within detection time

os_defaults.default: Triggered automatically on interval change via vtysh or CONFIG_DB update

timers.tuning_advice: Avoid rapidly changing BFD parameters on active sessions -- each change triggers a Poll sequence that can cause brief instability.

transitions.trigger: BFD control packet received from peer in DOWN state
BFD control packet received from peer in INIT or UP state
BFD control packet received from peer in INIT state (simultaneous establishment)

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Unidirectional forwarding failure, or peer BFD process crashed
transitions.trigger: Detection timer expires (no BFD packet received within detect time) or admin down received
Detection timer expires without peer transitioning to UP
Administrative enable of the BFD session -- operator removes 'shutdown' or re-enables BFD on the peer

transitions.failure_if_stuck: UDP port 3784 blocked, peer IP unreachable, wrong source IP, or CoPP rate-limiting BFD packets on either side
transitions.trigger: Received a BFD Control packet from the peer with State=Down or State=Init; bfdd transitions to Init to signal one-way reachability

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Persistent Down: peer is not transmitting BFD packets -- check peer bfdd process, IP reachability, and CoPP
transitions.trigger: Detection timer expires without receiving a valid Control packet from peer; session remains Down

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Stuck in Init: one-way reachability only -- check asymmetric routing (return path different), TTL issues for multi-hop, or CoPP dropping packets in one direction
transitions.trigger: Received a BFD Control packet from the peer with State=Init or State=Up; bidirectional reachability confirmed
Detection timer expires while in Init state -- peer stopped responding before confirming Up
Detection timer expires (no Control packets received within detect-multiplier * rx-interval), peer sends State=Down, or BFD Poll sequence failure
Operator disables the BFD session or shuts down the neighbor configuration

notes.note: BFD available without feature enable (bfd-template model or interface-level). Supports hardware offload (C-BFD) on Catalyst 9000. 'bfd interval <ms> min_rx <ms> multiplier <N>' configured under interface.

BFD configured under 'router ospf', 'router bgp', or 'router isis' with 'bfd minimum-interval <ms> multiplier <N>'. Supports 15ms minimum with hardware offload on Cisco 8000. Bundle-level BFD and per-member BFD (micro-BFD) supported.

BFD requires 'feature bfd'. Supports hardware offload on Nexus 9000 (50ms minimum). Configure per-protocol: 'ip ospf bfd', 'neighbor <ip> bfd', 'isis bfd'. Supports BFD over port-channel (per-link or micro-BFD).

relevant_fields: local_addr: source IP address for BFD packets (must match interface IP)
txinterval: desired min TX interval in milliseconds
rxinterval: required min RX interval in milliseconds
multiplier: detection time multiplier (default 3)
multihop: false for single-hop
echo_active: enable echo mode (true/false)
echo_transmit_interval: echo TX interval in milliseconds
shutdown: administratively disable the session (true/false)
local_addr: source loopback IP address for multi-hop BFD
txinterval: desired min TX interval in milliseconds
rxinterval: required min RX interval in milliseconds
multiplier: detection time multiplier
multihop: true
shutdown: administratively disable
state: current BFD session state (Up/Down/Init/AdminDown)
type: single_hop or multi_hop
local_addr: local IP address used for this session
peer_addr: remote peer IP address
local_discriminator: locally assigned BFD discriminator
remote_discriminator: peer's discriminator
local_diagnostic: diagnostic code for last state change
remote_diagnostic: peer's diagnostic code
local_state: local state as seen from this end
remote_state: peer's state as reported in last received packet
negotiated_transmit_interval: negotiated TX interval in microseconds
negotiated_receive_interval: negotiated RX interval in microseconds

frr_mapping.config_managed_by: frrcfgd or bgpcfgd translating CONFIG_DB BFD tables to FRR vtysh commands
frr_mapping.frr_config_file: /etc/frr/bfdd.conf (managed by frrcfgd or bgpcfgd; do not edit directly in SONiC)

key_commands: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers'
docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers counters'
docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show bfd peers brief'
docker exec bgp supervisorctl status bfdd
docker logs swss 2>&1 | grep -i bfd | tail -50

notes.note: BFD in SONiC runs as the bfdd process inside the 'bgp' Docker container, managed by FRR's integrated multi-daemon architecture. bfdd communicates with bgpd and ospfd via the ZAPI (Zebra API) socket. BFD configuration can be done via FRR vtysh ('bfd peer <ip>') or via CONFIG_DB BFD_PEER_SINGLE_HOP / BFD_PEER_MULTI_HOP tables. CONFIG_DB is the SONiC source of truth -- vtysh changes are overwritten on restart.

CoPP is the #1 cause of BFD false positives in SONiC production deployments. BFD packets are punted to the CPU by the ASIC and rate-limited by CoPP. The default BFD CoPP class rate may be insufficient for large numbers of sessions or aggressive timer intervals. Always check 'show copp statistics' for BFD class drops before assuming a physical failure caused a BFD session to go Down.
notes.source_ref: SONiC CoPP design and platform-specific CoPP configs in sonic-buildimage

notes.note: BFD session state in SONiC is reflected in STATE_DB (Redis DB 6) in the BFD_SESSION_TABLE. This is the correct place to check BFD state programmatically or for monitoring integration. The 'show bfd peers' CLI command reads from STATE_DB. Direct redis-cli queries to DB 6 provide raw session details including diagnostic codes.
notes.source_ref: SONiC STATE_DB schema and bfdd state publication via ZAPI

notes.note: When BGP uses BFD ('neighbor <ip> bfd' in FRR), BFD session Down events cause immediate BGP session teardown -- faster than the BGP Hold Timer. This is the intended behavior but operators sometimes see BGP sessions dropping unexpectedly and assume a BGP problem when the root cause is a BFD false positive (CPU overload, CoPP drop). Always correlate BFD session events with BGP adjacency changes when troubleshooting BGP flaps.
notes.source_ref: FRR bgpd BFD integration and SONiC BGP+BFD design

notes.note: BFD timer negotiation: the actual TX interval used is max(local desired-min-tx, remote required-min-rx). The actual detection time = remote detect-multiplier * max(local desired-min-tx, remote required-min-rx). Configuring 50ms on a local peer does nothing if the remote requires 300ms RX -- detection will be 300ms * remote-multiplier. Check 'show bfd peers' negotiated interval fields, not just configured values.
notes.source_ref: RFC 5880 Section 6.8.7 -- Transmitting BFD Control Packets

notes.note: Multi-hop BFD (UDP port 4784) requires the local-address to be explicitly configured when the source IP must be a loopback. Without explicit local-address, bfdd may source packets from the wrong interface IP, causing the peer to drop packets that don't match the expected source. Always configure 'local-address <loopback-ip>' for multi-hop BFD sessions used with BGP loopback peering.
notes.source_ref: RFC 5883 and FRR bfdd multi-hop peer configuration

notes.note: Echo mode is rarely used in SONiC data-center deployments. It requires the peer's forwarding plane to loop back packets, which is not supported by all platforms or virtual environments. Disable echo mode ('no bfd echo-mode' in FRR or echo_active: false in CONFIG_DB) unless explicitly required and peer support has been verified. Echo mode failures produce misleading diagnostics because Control packet exchange is healthy while echo detection fails.
notes.source_ref: RFC 5880 Section 6.4 -- Echo Function and FRR bfdd echo implementation

notes.note: Hardware-offloaded BFD is platform-specific and not universally available in SONiC. On supported platforms (some Broadcom and NVIDIA/Mellanox ASICs), orchagent programs BFD sessions into hardware via SAI, allowing the ASIC to maintain liveness independently of the control plane CPU. When hardware offload is available, it improves BFD reliability under CPU load. Check 'redis-cli -n 1 KEYS ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_BFD_SESSION*' to see if hardware offload is active.
notes.source_ref: SONiC BFD HW Offload design document and orchagent/bfdorch.cpp
